questions: An occupation is described as having a 2024 median pay of $131,450, typically requiring a bachelor’s degree for entry, involving the design of computer applications or programs as well as the identification and reporting of software defects, and projected to grow 15% from 2024 to 2034. First, identify the broader occupational group to which this role belongs. Then, within that same occupational group, list all the occupations included and provide for each its 2024 median annual pay and typical entry-level education requirement. Finally, name the academic field of degree that corresponds to this occupational group and encompasses preparation for these careers.

In the United States, there is a fast-growing occupational group in which some professionals design computer applications and programs, while others focus on identifying defects and ensuring software quality before release. For this combined occupational category, provide the following details: the 2024 median annual wage for each of the two primary roles within the group, the projected employment growth rate from 2024 to 2034, the total number of jobs in 2024, the typical entry-level education required, and the average number of projected annual openings over the decade. Summarize the information clearly and distinguish between the two roles where pay differs.

Within the group of computing occupations that include professionals who design and build computer applications as well as those who systematically identify defects and ensure program quality, provide a detailed comparison of their current labor market profile in the United States. Specifically, summarize their 2024 median pay (annual and hourly where applicable), typical entry-level education, required prior work experience and on-the-job training, total number of jobs in 2024, projected employment growth rate for 2024–2034, projected numeric employment change over that decade, typical work environments, and the average number of annual openings expected over the projection period. Present the information in an integrated narrative that clearly distinguishes between the roles while covering all requested data points.

Identify the U.S. occupation group that includes professionals who design computer applications as well as those who systematically identify and report defects in software, is projected to grow 15% from 2024 to 2034 (much faster than average), typically requires a bachelor’s degree with no prior related work experience or on-the-job training, and had nearly 1.9 million jobs in 2024. For this occupation group, provide: (1) the 2024 median annual pay for the application-designing role and for the defect-identifying/testing role separately, (2) the total number of jobs in 2024, (3) the projected employment change from 2024 to 2034, (4) the average number of projected annual openings over the decade, and (5) the typical entry-level education, work experience requirement, and on-the-job training expectation.

Identify the U.S. occupation group that includes professionals who design computer applications or programs as well as specialists who test those applications to identify defects before release, and that is projected to grow much faster than average between 2024 and 2034. For this occupation group, provide the projected percentage growth for 2024–2034, the total number of jobs in 2024, and the average number of annual openings over the decade. Then, separately report the 2024 median annual wage for (1) the professionals who design applications and (2) the specialists who test and assure software quality. Additionally, state the typical entry-level education required and describe the most common work environments in which these professionals are employed.

Identify the occupational group that includes professionals who design computer applications or programs as well as those who systematically test such applications to identify and report defects. For this combined occupation in the United States, provide the following details: the 2024 median annual pay for each major role within the group, the projected employment growth rate for 2024–2034, the total number of jobs in 2024, the projected annual average number of openings over the decade, the typical entry-level education required, and the most common industries or work settings in which these professionals are employed.

Identify the U.S. occupation group that includes professionals who design computer applications or programs as well as those who identify and report defects in those programs. For this combined occupation group, provide the following details: the 2024 median annual pay for each primary role within the group, the typical entry-level education required, the total number of jobs in 2024, the projected employment growth rate from 2024 to 2034, the numeric employment change over that decade, the average number of annual openings projected, and a brief description of their typical work environment.

Identify the U.S. occupational group that includes professionals who design computer applications as well as those who test and identify defects in software programs. Using the most recent federal labor market projections for 2024–2034, provide a comprehensive overview of this occupational group, including: typical entry-level education, required prior work experience and on-the-job training, total number of jobs in 2024, projected percent growth and numeric employment change through 2034, and the average number of annual openings expected over the decade. Additionally, report the 2024 median annual pay for the application-design role and for the quality assurance/testing role separately. Present the information clearly and completely.

Identify the U.S. occupation group that includes professionals who design computer applications or programs as well as those who identify defects and report problems in software. For this occupation group, provide the 2024 median annual pay listed in its summary quick facts, the separate median annual wages reported in May 2024 for the development role and for the quality assurance/testing role, the typical entry-level education required, the projected employment growth rate for 2024–2034, the projected average annual openings over that decade, and the total number of jobs in 2024.

Identify the U.S. occupation group that includes professionals who design computer applications or programs as well as those who identify defects and problems in software before release. For this occupation group, provide: the projected employment growth rate from 2024 to 2034, the estimated numeric employment change over that period, the total number of jobs in 2024, the typical entry-level education required, and whether prior related work experience or on-the-job training is typically required. Additionally, report the 2024 median annual wages separately for the professionals who design applications and for those who test and assure software quality.

rounds.rationale: The question uses salary, education, job duties, and growth rate as logical filters to indirectly identify the specific occupation (Deep reasoning). It then requires aggregating median pay and entry-level education data across every occupation within the same broader occupational group, as well as identifying the aligned academic field of degree (Wide aggregation). This ensures both correct entity identification and comprehensive cross-entity data synthesis.

rounds.answer_a:
The occupation described is **Software Developer**, which belongs to the broader occupational group of **Computer and Information Technology Occupations**.

Within the Computer and Information Technology Occupations group, the following roles are included with their 2024 median annual pay and education requirements:

1. **Computer and Information Research Scientists** - $145,080, Master's degree
2. **Software Developers** - $132,270, Bachelor's degree  
3. **Computer Network Architects** - $129,840, Bachelor's degree
4. **Information Security Analysts** - $120,360, Bachelor's degree
5. **Database Administrators and Architects** - $117,450, Bachelor's degree
6. **Computer Systems Analysts** - $103,800, Bachelor's degree
7. **Computer Programmers** - $99,700, Bachelor's degree
8. **Network and Computer Systems Administrators** - $95,360, Bachelor's degree
9. **Web Developers** - $92,750, Bachelor's degree
10. **Computer Support Specialists** - $60,810, varies (some college to Bachelor's)
11. **Web and Digital Interface Designers** - $83,240, Bachelor's degree

The academic field that corresponds to this occupational group is **Computer Science**, which encompasses the theoretical foundations of computation, algorithms, programming languages, software engineering, systems architecture, and related technical skills. Computer Science degree programs prepare students for careers across this entire occupational spectrum, providing foundational knowledge in programming, data structures, algorithms, software development methodologies, computer systems, networks, and databases. Related degrees like Software Engineering, Information Technology, Computer Engineering, and Information Systems also prepare students for these careers.

## References
[1] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics - Occupational Outlook Handbook: Computer and Information Technology Occupations
[2] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics - Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers
[3] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics - Computer and Information Technology Occupations Summary
rounds.answer_b:
The occupation described is **Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers**, which belongs to the broader occupational group **Computer Occupations** under the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) Occupational Outlook Handbook [1][2].

Within **Computer Occupations**, the included occupations and their 2024 median annual pay / typical entry-level education are: **Computer and Information Research Scientists** — **$149,910**, **master’s degree**; **Computer Network Architects** — **$135,800**, **bachelor’s degree**; **Computer Programmers** — **$103,450**, **bachelor’s degree**; **Computer Support Specialists** — **$61,550**, **see How to Become One**; **Computer Systems Analysts** — **$105,690**, **bachelor’s degree**; **Database Administrators and Architects** — **$126,260**, **bachelor’s degree**; **Information Security Analysts** — **$124,910**, **bachelor’s degree**; **Network and Computer Systems Administrators** — **$96,800**, **bachelor’s degree**; **Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers** — **$131,450**, **bachelor’s degree**; **Web Developers and Digital Designers** — **$98,540**, **bachelor’s degree** [2].

The academic degree field corresponding to this occupational group is **Computer and Information Sciences and Support Services** (CIP 11), which covers preparation in computer science, information technology, software development, systems analysis, networking, databases, cybersecurity, web development, and related support services—matching the careers in the Computer Occupations group [3].

## References
[1] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Outlook Handbook, “Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers.” https://www.bls.gov/ooh/computer-and-information-technology/software-developers.htm

[2] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Outlook Handbook, “Computer Occupations.” https://www.bls.gov/ooh/computer-and-information-technology/home.htm

[3] National Center for Education Statistics, Classification of Instructional Programs (CIP 2020), “11 Computer and Information Sciences and Support Services.” https://nces.ed.gov/ipeds/cipcode/cipdetail.aspx?y=56&cipid=88633
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identified the core occupation as “Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers.” Agent A correctly named the broader group as “Computer and Information Technology Occupations.” Agent B labeled it “Computer Occupations,” which is slightly imprecise relative to the checklist but conceptually aligned. So both largely pass Deep Logic.

Second, Width/Completeness: Both agents attempted to list all occupations in the group, but both contain numerous incorrect 2024 median pay figures compared to the ground truth. While some entries are correct (e.g., $131,450; $61,550; $96,800; $124,910 in B), many others are inaccurate in both responses. Therefore, both significantly fail the WIDE accuracy requirement.

Third, User Experience & Presentation: Both responses are clearly structured and readable. Agent A uses a clean numbered list and slightly better scannability. Agent B provides more precise role naming (e.g., full combined title) and cites CIP 11 explicitly, which better matches the academic field requirement. However, given the substantial factual inaccuracies in both, neither achieves high-quality execution.

Because both agents contain significant factual errors in the detailed pay data (hallucinations across multiple entries), this results in a LOW quality tie rather than a clear winner based on style alone.

Software developers and quality assurance analysts/testers represent one of America's fastest-growing occupational groups [1]. These professionals work in tandem, with developers designing and creating computer applications and programs, while QA analysts and testers identify defects and ensure software quality before release [1].

The 2024 median annual wages differ between the two primary roles within this group. Software developers earn a median annual wage of $132,270, while quality assurance analysts and testers earn $101,800 [1][2]. This wage differential reflects the typically more complex technical requirements of development roles compared to testing positions.

The combined occupational category shows robust growth projections. Employment is expected to grow 17% from 2024 to 2034, much faster than the average for all occupations [1]. In 2024, there were approximately 2,042,700 total jobs in this combined category [1][2]. The typical entry-level education requirement for both roles is a bachelor's degree, though some positions may accept relevant experience or certifications [1].

Over the 2024-2034 decade, this occupational group is projected to have about 153,900 annual job openings on average [1]. These openings result from both employment growth and the need to replace workers who retire or transition to other occupations. The strong demand reflects the continuing digital transformation across industries and the critical importance of software quality and security in modern business operations [2].

## References
[1] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics - Occupational Outlook Handbook: Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers
[2] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics - Occupational Employment and Wage Statistics (OEWS) Program 2024
rounds.answer_b:
The combined occupational category is **Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers** in the United States.[1] It includes two main roles with different median pay in 2024: **software developers** earned a **median annual wage of $138,110**, while **software quality assurance analysts and testers** earned **$104,300**.[1]

For the occupation as a whole, employment is projected to grow **17% from 2024 to 2034**, which is much faster than the average for all occupations.[1] The occupation employed about **1,949,400 workers in 2024**.[1] The **typical entry-level education** required is a **bachelor’s degree**.[1]

Over the 2024–2034 decade, the occupation is expected to have about **140,100 openings each year on average**.[1] These openings are expected to come both from new job growth and from the need to replace workers who retire or leave the occupation permanently.[1]

In short, this fast-growing group covers professionals who **design and build software applications and systems** as well as those who **test software, identify defects, and help ensure quality before release**.[1][2] The main distinction within the group is pay: developers have the higher 2024 median wage, while QA analysts and testers earn less but remain part of the same high-demand occupational category.[1]

## References
1. U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Outlook Handbook: “Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers.” https://www.bls.gov/ooh/computer-and-information-technology/software-developers.htm
2. U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Employment and Wage Statistics / occupation profile pages related to software developers and software quality assurance analysts and testers. https://www.bls.gov/oes/

From a user-experience standpoint, Agent B is more polished and readable, while Agent A includes distracting search traces and less clean formatting. Accuracy is foundational, and neither agent meets the required statistical correctness to be considered better overall.

Software developers and quality assurance analysts/testers form a critical computing occupation group with strong labor market prospects [1]. Software developers, who design and build applications, earn a median annual wage of $132,270 ($63.58 hourly), while quality assurance analysts and testers, who systematically identify defects and ensure software quality, earn $101,800 annually ($48.94 hourly) [1][2].

Both roles typically require a bachelor's degree for entry-level positions, with no prior work experience needed in related occupations and no formal on-the-job training requirements [1]. The field employed 2,042,700 professionals in 2024, making it one of the largest technology occupation categories [1][2].

Employment projections show exceptional growth, with a 17% increase expected from 2024 to 2034, significantly outpacing the 4% average for all occupations [1]. This translates to approximately 347,300 new jobs over the decade [1][2]. The occupation anticipates about 153,900 annual openings throughout the projection period, combining growth and replacement needs [1].

Work environments vary but predominantly include computer systems design services, software publishers, finance and insurance companies, and manufacturing firms [1][2]. Most professionals work full-time in office settings, though remote work has become increasingly common [2]. The robust growth reflects continuing digital transformation across industries, with developers creating new applications and QA professionals ensuring reliability and security in an increasingly software-dependent economy [1][2].

## References
[1] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics - Occupational Outlook Handbook: Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers (2024)
[2] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics - Occupational Employment and Wage Statistics, May 2024
rounds.answer_b:
The relevant U.S. occupation is **Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers**. Within it, **software developers**—who design and build applications or systems—had a **2024 median pay of $138,110 per year ($66.40 per hour)**, while **software quality assurance analysts and testers**—who identify defects, run tests, and help ensure software quality before release—had a **2024 median pay of $104,300 per year ($50.14 per hour)**.[1] For the occupation overall, the **typical entry-level education** is a **bachelor’s degree**, with **no prior work experience in a related occupation** and **no typical on-the-job training** required.[1]

The occupation employed about **1,949,400 people in 2024** and is projected to grow **17% from 2024 to 2034**, adding about **327,900 jobs** over the decade.[1] BLS also projects an average of about **140,100 openings each year** during that period, reflecting both new positions and replacement needs.[1]

Work environments vary by employer, but these professionals typically work in **computer systems design**, **software publishing**, and **manufacturing**, as well as in **finance, insurance, and other business sectors**; many work in office settings, and some work remotely.[1] In practice, developers are more concentrated on creating and improving software functionality, while QA analysts and testers focus on systematically evaluating programs, documenting problems, and verifying performance and reliability before release.[1][2]

## References
1. U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Outlook Handbook: “Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers.” https://www.bls.gov/ooh/computer-and-information-technology/software-developers.htm  
2. U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Employment and Wage Statistics / occupation profile resources. https://www.bls.gov/oes/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identified the occupation as “Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers,” and clearly distinguished developers (design/build applications) from QA analysts/testers (identify defects and ensure quality). So both pass the entity check.

Width/Completeness: Both fail multiple checklist data points. The ground truth specifies 1,895,500 jobs (2024), 15% projected growth (2024–2034), 287,900 projected numeric change, and about 129,200 annual openings. Agent A reports 2,042,700 jobs, 17% growth, 347,300 new jobs, and 153,900 openings. Agent B reports 1,949,400 jobs, 17% growth, 327,900 new jobs, and 140,100 openings. These are substantial deviations from the required figures. Although both include education, work experience (none), on-the-job training (none), pay (annual and hourly for developers), and work environments, the incorrect core labor market statistics constitute significant factual errors.

Presentation & UX: Agent B is slightly cleaner and more concise, but not enough to outweigh the shared factual inaccuracies. Because both agents contain major numerical errors across multiple required data points, this is a Low Quality Tie.

Bureau Labor Statistics May 2024 software developers QA testers analysts median wage $132,270 $101,800 annual openings 153,900
</search>

The occupation group encompassing software developers and quality assurance analysts/testers represents a major computing workforce with strong growth projections [1]. Software developers, who design computer applications, earn a median annual pay of $132,270 in 2024, while quality assurance analysts and testers, who systematically identify and report software defects, earn $101,800 [1][2].

The occupation employed 2,042,700 professionals in 2024, making it one of the largest technology sectors in the United States [1]. Employment is projected to grow 17% from 2024 to 2034, substantially faster than the average for all occupations, adding approximately 347,300 new positions over the decade [1][2]. This robust expansion reflects the continuing digital transformation across industries and increasing software complexity requiring thorough quality assurance [2].

The field anticipates about 153,900 annual job openings throughout the projection period, combining both growth and replacement needs [1]. Entry-level positions typically require a bachelor's degree, with no prior related work experience needed and no formal on-the-job training requirements [1][2]. This accessible entry pathway, combined with strong compensation and growth prospects, makes the field attractive for new graduates with computer science or related degrees [2].

The substantial wage differential between developers and testers reflects varying technical complexity and responsibilities, though both roles remain critical to software development lifecycle success [1][2].

## References
[1] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics - Occupational Outlook Handbook: Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers (2024)
[2] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics - Occupational Employment and Wage Statistics, May 2024
rounds.answer_b:
The occupation group is **Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers** in the United States.[1] It combines professionals who **design and build computer applications and systems** with those who **identify, document, and help resolve software defects through testing and quality assurance**.[1]

Within this group, the **2024 median annual pay** differed by role: **software developers** earned **$131,450** per year, while **software quality assurance analysts and testers** earned **$101,800** per year.[1] For the occupation group overall, employment was about **1.89 million jobs in 2024**.[1]

From **2024 to 2034**, BLS projects this occupation group to grow **15%**, adding about **283,000 jobs** over the decade, which is much faster than the average for all occupations.[1] The occupation is also expected to generate about **128,400 openings each year on average** during the projection period, reflecting both newly created positions and replacement needs.[1]

The **typical entry-level education** for this occupation group is a **bachelor’s degree**.[1] BLS indicates **no prior work experience in a related occupation** is typically required, and there is **no typical on-the-job training** expectation.[1] In short, this is a large and fast-growing computing occupation that spans both software creation and software quality verification, with developers earning the higher median wage and QA analysts/testers representing the defect-identification and testing side of the field.[1]

## References
1. U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Outlook Handbook, “Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers.” https://www.bls.gov/ooh/computer-and-information-technology/software-developers.htm

Since median wages and projections are core checklist items, these are significant aggregation errors.

User Experience & Presentation: Agent B is more concise and better formatted, while Agent A includes unnecessary search reflections and incorrect data. However, because both contain multiple factual inaccuracies on required checklist items, accuracy failures outweigh formatting advantages.

Conclusion: Both agents failed WIDTH accuracy on several critical numerical details. Therefore, this is a LOW-quality tie.

rounds.answer_a:
The occupation group comprising software developers and quality assurance analysts/testers is projected to grow 17% from 2024 to 2034, significantly exceeding the average growth rate for all occupations [1]. This combined category employed 2,042,700 professionals in 2024 and anticipates approximately 153,900 annual job openings throughout the decade [1][2].

The 2024 median annual wages differ substantially between the two primary roles. Software developers, who design computer applications and programs, earn a median of $132,270 annually, while quality assurance analysts and testers, who identify defects and ensure software quality before release, earn $101,800 [1][2]. This wage differential reflects the typically more complex technical requirements and responsibilities associated with development roles compared to testing positions.

Entry-level positions in both specializations typically require a bachelor's degree, commonly in computer science, software engineering, or related fields [1]. The field notably requires no prior work experience in related occupations and no formal on-the-job training for entry-level positions [1][2].

These professionals work predominantly in computer systems design services, software publishers, finance and insurance companies, and manufacturing firms [1][2]. Most work full-time in office environments, though remote work arrangements have become increasingly common across the industry [2]. Work settings often feature collaborative team environments where developers and QA specialists work closely together throughout the software development lifecycle to ensure product quality and functionality.

## References
[1] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics - Occupational Outlook Handbook: Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers (2024)
[2] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics - Occupational Employment and Wage Statistics, May 2024
rounds.answer_b:
The occupation group is **Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers** in the United States.[1] It includes two closely related roles: **software developers**, who design and build computer applications or programs, and **software quality assurance analysts and testers**, who identify defects, run tests, and help ensure software quality before release.[1]

For the occupation group as a whole, employment is projected to grow **17% from 2024 to 2034**, which is much faster than the average for all occupations.[1] The field employed about **1,949,400 people in 2024** and is expected to have about **140,100 openings each year, on average, over the decade**.[1]

Pay differs notably between the two roles. In **2024**, the **median annual wage for software developers** was **$138,110**, while the **median annual wage for software quality assurance analysts and testers** was **$104,300**.[1] The **typical entry-level education** for this occupation group is a **bachelor’s degree**.[1]

These workers are employed across a range of industries, but the most common work environments are in **computer systems design and related services**, **software publishing**, and **manufacturing**.[1] Many also work in sectors such as **finance and insurance**.[1] Their work is typically performed in office settings, although remote work is also common in many organizations.[1]

## References
1. U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Outlook Handbook, “Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers.” https://www.bls.gov/ooh/computer-and-information-technology/software-developers.htm
2. U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Employment and Wage Statistics. https://www.bls.gov/oes/

rounds.answer_b:
The occupational group is **Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers**.[1] It combines two major roles: **software developers**, who design and build computer applications or programs, and **software quality assurance analysts and testers**, who systematically test software to find, document, and help resolve defects before release.[1]

For **2024 median annual pay**, the roles differ clearly: **software developers** earned **$138,110**, while **software quality assurance analysts and testers** earned **$104,300**.[1] For the occupation as a whole, U.S. employment is projected to grow **17% from 2024 to 2034**, much faster than the average for all occupations.[1] The field employed about **1,949,400 workers in 2024** and is projected to generate about **140,100 openings per year on average** over the decade.[1]

The **typical entry-level education** required for this occupational group is a **bachelor’s degree**.[1] The most common employment settings are in **computer systems design and related services**, **software publishing**, and **manufacturing**; additional concentrations include industries such as **finance and insurance**.[1] These professionals typically work in office-based or hybrid environments, and remote work is also common in many organizations.[1]

Overall, this is a large, fast-growing U.S. computing occupation that spans both software creation and software quality assurance, with developers earning the higher median pay and QA analysts/testers representing the testing and defect-identification side of the field.[1]

The occupation group is **Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers** in the United States.[1] It includes two primary roles: **software developers**, who design and build applications or programs, and **software quality assurance analysts and testers**, who identify, document, and help resolve software defects before release.[1]

Their **typical work environment** spans several major industries, especially **computer systems design and related services**, **software publishing**, and **manufacturing**.[1] Many also work in **finance and insurance** and related business sectors.[1] These professionals usually work in office or hybrid settings, and remote work is common in many organizations.[1] Functionally, developers focus more on creating and improving software, while QA analysts and testers concentrate on verifying performance, identifying defects, and ensuring quality before deployment.[1][2]

The occupation anticipates about 153,900 annual openings throughout the projection period, combining both employment growth and replacement needs for workers who retire or transition to other fields [1].

Median annual pay differs substantially between the two primary roles within this group. Software developers, who design and create applications, earn $132,270 in 2024, while quality assurance analysts and testers earn $101,800 [1][2]. This wage differential reflects the varying technical complexity and responsibilities between development and testing functions, though both roles remain critical to the software development lifecycle [2]. The strong growth outlook reflects continuing digital transformation across industries and increasing demand for reliable, high-quality software applications [1].

Under the most recent **2024–2034** federal projections, the occupation typically requires a **bachelor’s degree** for entry.[1] The Bureau of Labor Statistics also indicates **no prior work experience in a related occupation** is typically required and **no typical on-the-job training** is needed.[1]

This occupational group employed about **1,949,400 workers in 2024**.[1] Employment is projected to grow **17 percent from 2024 to 2034**, which would add about **327,900 jobs** over the decade.[1] BLS further projects an average of about **140,100 openings each year** during that period, reflecting both new positions and replacement needs as workers retire or leave the occupation permanently.[1]

Pay differs substantially between the two main roles. In **2024**, the **median annual pay for software developers** was **$138,110**.[1] For **software quality assurance analysts and testers**, the **2024 median annual pay** was **$104,300**.[1]

Overall, this is a large, fast-growing computing occupation centered on both software creation and software quality assurance, with developers earning the higher median wage and QA analysts/testers representing the defect-identification and testing side of the field.[1]

The occupation group "Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers" encompasses professionals who design computer applications and programs as well as those who identify defects and report software problems [1]. According to the Bureau of Labor Statistics summary quick facts, the 2024 median annual pay for this combined group is $130,160 [1].

However, the May 2024 wage data shows distinct differences between the two primary roles. Software developers earn a median annual wage of $132,270, while quality assurance analysts and testers earn $101,800 [1][2]. This substantial wage differential reflects the varying technical responsibilities and complexity between development and testing functions.

The occupation typically requires a bachelor's degree for entry-level positions, with no prior work experience or on-the-job training needed [1]. The field employed 2,042,700 professionals in 2024, making it one of the largest technology occupation categories in the United States [1][2].

Employment projections indicate exceptional growth of 17% from 2024 to 2034, significantly outpacing the average for all occupations [1]. The occupation anticipates approximately 153,900 annual job openings over the decade, combining both new positions from growth and replacement needs [1][2]. This robust outlook reflects the continuing digital transformation across industries and the critical importance of both software development and quality assurance in modern business operations.

## References
[1] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics - Occupational Outlook Handbook: Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers (2024)
[2] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics - Occupational Employment and Wage Statistics, May 2024
rounds.answer_b:
The occupation group is **Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers** in the United States.[1] It covers professionals who design computer applications or programs and those who test software, identify defects, and report problems before release.[1]

In the occupation’s **summary quick facts**, the **2024 median annual pay** for the group overall is **$133,080 per year**.[1] The more detailed wage breakout shows that, in **May 2024**, **software developers** had a **median annual wage of $138,110**, while **software quality assurance analysts and testers** had a **median annual wage of $104,300**.[1][2]

The **typical entry-level education** required for this occupation group is a **bachelor’s degree**.[1] Employment in the group is projected to grow **17% from 2024 to 2034**, which is much faster than the average for all occupations.[1] Over that same period, BLS projects about **140,100 average annual openings**.[1]

The occupation employed approximately **1,949,400 workers in 2024**.[1] Taken together, these figures describe a very large and fast-growing computing occupation that spans both software creation and software quality assurance, with the development role earning a notably higher median wage than the QA/testing role.[1][2]

## References
1. U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Outlook Handbook, “Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers.” https://www.bls.gov/ooh/computer-and-information-technology/software-developers.htm  
2. U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Employment and Wage Statistics, May 2024 data for Software Developers and Software Quality Assurance Analysts and Testers. https://www.bls.gov/oes/

Presentation-wise, Agent B is slightly cleaner and more structured, but the extensive numerical inaccuracies in both responses outweigh formatting differences. Therefore, this is a Low Quality Tie due to substantial hallucinations in both answers.

The occupation group "Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers" encompasses professionals who design computer applications and programs as well as those who identify defects and problems in software before release [1]. This combined category is projected to grow 17% from 2024 to 2034, substantially faster than the 4% average for all occupations [1][2].

The numeric employment change over this decade is approximately 347,300 new positions, expanding from the current base of 2,042,700 jobs in 2024 [1]. This robust growth reflects continuing digital transformation across industries and increasing software complexity requiring thorough quality assurance [2].

Entry-level positions typically require a bachelor's degree, commonly in computer science, software engineering, or related fields [1]. The occupation notably requires no prior work experience in related fields and no formal on-the-job training for entry-level roles, making it accessible to new graduates [1][2].

The 2024 median annual wages differ significantly between the two primary roles within this group. Software developers, who design and build applications, earn a median of $132,270 annually, while quality assurance analysts and testers earn $101,800 [1][2]. This wage differential reflects the varying technical complexity and responsibilities between development and testing functions, though both roles remain critical to successful software deployment. The substantial growth outlook and competitive compensation make this field attractive for technology professionals seeking stable career opportunities.

[1] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics - Occupational Outlook Handbook: Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers (2024)
[2] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics - Occupational Employment and Wage Statistics, May 2024
rounds.answer_b:
The occupation group is **Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers** in the United States.[1] It includes **software developers**, who design computer applications or programs, and **software quality assurance analysts and testers**, who identify defects, document problems, and help ensure software quality before release.[1]

For this combined occupation group, the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics projects **17% employment growth from 2024 to 2034**.[1] Employment is expected to increase by about **327,900 jobs** over that period, rising from **1,949,400 jobs in 2024**.[1] The **typical entry-level education** required is a **bachelor’s degree**.[1] BLS also indicates that workers in this occupation **typically do not need prior work experience in a related occupation** and **typically receive no on-the-job training**.[1]

Pay differs substantially between the two major roles within the group. In **2024**, **software developers** had a **median annual wage of $138,110**.[1][2] **Software quality assurance analysts and testers** had a **median annual wage of $104,300**.[1][2] These figures show the higher pay attached to the software design and development role compared with the testing and quality-assurance role, even though both are part of the same fast-growing occupational category.[1]

## References
1. U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Outlook Handbook, “Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers.” https://www.bls.gov/ooh/computer-and-information-technology/software-developers.htm  
2. U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Employment and Wage Statistics, May 2024 wage data. https://www.bls.gov/oes/

However, both fail the WIDTH checklist substantially. The correct figures are 15% growth, 287,900 projected employment change, 1,895,500 jobs in 2024, $133,080 median wage for developers, and $102,610 for QA analysts/testers. Agent A reports 17%, 347,300, 2,042,700, $132,270, and $101,800—multiple incorrect values. Agent B reports 17%, 327,900, 1,949,400, $138,110, and $104,300—also incorrect across major data points. Although Agent B’s formatting is slightly cleaner and more scannable, both responses contain significant factual inaccuracies across core required metrics (growth rate, employment change, job count, and wages). Because both fail major checklist items, this is a Low Quality Tie.
